steps.text: Determine whether the licensee conducted experiments in accordance with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments, since the last inspection.
Determine whether the scope of experiments conducted at the facility were consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine whether the licensee's procedures for the control and conduct of experiments are consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.

Determine whether the licensee reviewed and approved experiments performed at the facility, and any subsequent changes made to the experiments, in accordance with Technical Specification (TS) requirements and the licensee's procedural requirements.
Determine whether the licensee reviewed experiments for TS changes or compliance to 10 CFR 50.59.
Determine whether the licensee identified hazards associated with the experiments conducted since the last inspection and took appropriate remedial action.
Determine whether the licensee predicted the approximate reactivity effect on the reactor before conducting the experiment.
Determine whether the licensee confirmed the reactivity effect as required by the TS.

Determine whether the licensee accounted for irradiated items until disposing of them or until they decayed to an acceptable level, in accordance with regulatory requirements and applicable licensee procedures.
Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as required by the TS.
Determine whether researchers adhered to the licensees procedural controls when loading experiments into the test facilities and removing them from the test facilities.
Determine whether the experiments reduced the operator’s ability to control reactivity manipulations or the protective system’s ability to shut down the reactor if needed.
Determine whether engineered radiation safety controls were used during experiments and posting was accomplished as required.
Examine new experiments and changes to the licensee’s experiments and related programs since the last inspection.
Review 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Review documentation including the Annual Report or experiment logs prior to the onsite inspection.
Review licensee’s procedures for the control and conduct of experiments prior to the onsite inspection.
Conduct direct observation of 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Review the information available to the operator that authorizes the conduct of a particular experiment.
Approve routine experiments such as gold foil irradiation by the reactor supervisor, an SRO, or the radiation control officer.
Document routine experiments for each new group of experimenters, or after one calendar year or more since last performance.
Ensure experiments pose no hazard to the reactor, personnel, or the public before approval.
Approve experiments that could pose a hazard to the safety of the reactor, personnel, or the public by the safety review committee.
Verify that all expressed conditions are met before conducting experiments approved by the safety review committee.
Approve experiments with significant hazard potential by the safety review committee, reactor supervisor, an SRO, and the radiation control officer.
Insert, disassemble, or remove experiments under the supervision of the reactor supervisor or a duly authorized SRO.
Provide a written description of reactor operations from insertion until removal before inserting an experiment in the reactor.

constraints.text: Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as required by the TS.
Experiments must pose no hazard to the reactor, personnel, or the public.
The use of explosives requires NRC review and approval.
Operators and experimenters must understand the hazards associated with an experiment and the methods to identify and respond to them.
The operator must detect any large change in reactivity during an experiment.
The need for constraints will depend on the type of reactor, and will be specifically stated in the TS.
An experiment can be considered constrained if it has a large mass or heavy shielding that minimize potential movement.
Secure arrangements should be established so that a single movement does not pulse a non-pulsing reactor, or exceed TS requirements or safety analysis assumptions.
Experiments that are of negative reactivity on insertion into the reactor core, are positive on withdrawal and must meet TS requirements.
